instruction: I paid $217.80 for ChatGPT Pro through Google Play. Is this a business expense?
response: Yes, if you use ChatGPT Pro for business purposes like software development, content creation, or research. It's a Schedule C deduction under 'Software and Cloud Services.' Keep the Google Play receipt as documentation. The full $217.80 is deductible if 100% business use.

instruction: I have a $100/month phone bill from AT&T and I use my phone to code and manage my business. Can I deduct it?
response: You can deduct the business-use percentage of your phone bill. If you estimate 75% business use (coding, GitHub management, AI agent testing, email), you can deduct $75/month or $900/year. Document your business use — apps installed, work calls, development tools on the phone. The IRS accepts reasonable estimates.

instruction: I sold Bitcoin through Cash App and lost $79.25 total for the year. How do I report this?
response: Report on Schedule D and Form 8949. Cash App should provide a 1099-B or gain/loss CSV using FIFO method. Short-term capital losses (held less than 1 year) offset short-term gains first, then long-term gains, then up to $3,000 of ordinary income. Your $79.25 loss reduces your taxable income by $79.25. If Cash App provides the CSV, import it directly into your tax software.

instruction: I spent $588 on Pipedream annual plan plus $240 in overage credits for workflow automation. Is this deductible?
response: Yes, both the annual subscription ($588) and usage overage charges ($240) are deductible as business software expenses on Schedule C. Workflow automation tools used for business operations are standard business expenses. Total deduction: $828. Keep the Stripe receipts and invoices from Pipedream.

instruction: I make about $750 biweekly from my W-2 job and spend about $1,200/month on AI subscriptions for my startup. My business has no profit yet. Can I still deduct the expenses?
response: Yes. A business doesn't need to be profitable to deduct expenses, especially in its early years. The IRS allows business losses on Schedule C that offset your W-2 income, resulting in a lower tax bill or refund. However, the IRS requires you to show profit intent — having a patent pending, published products, a business website, and active revenue attempts (Shopify, Gumroad, Stripe) all demonstrate this. The general rule is profit in 3 of 5 years, but startups get more leeway. Document everything.

instruction: I paid for Everweave, an AI DnD game subscription ($150+). The game logs became the seed corpus for my patent-pending AI safety system. Is this deductible?
response: This is arguable as a research expense. The key is documentation: if you can show that the 12,596 paragraphs of game logs directly became the training data for your patent-pending system (USPTO #63/961,403), it's a legitimate R&D expense. Keep records showing the connection — the game logs, the tokenizer seed derivation, and the patent application referencing this origin. Classify under 'Research and Development' on Schedule C.

instruction: I have stock trades on Cash App — 236 transactions across 65 tickers with a net realized loss of $2,211. How do I handle this?
response: Report all realized gains and losses on Schedule D using Form 8949. Cash App should provide a 1099-B or CSV. The net $2,211 short-term capital loss offsets other income: first any capital gains, then up to $3,000 of ordinary income (W-2, etc). If your total capital loss exceeds $3,000, carry the excess forward to next year. Since yours is $2,211, you can deduct it all this year. Unrealized positions (stocks you still hold) are not reported until you sell.

instruction: What's the difference between a business expense and a capital expense for software subscriptions?
response: Monthly software subscriptions (ChatGPT, GitHub Copilot, Notion, etc.) are ordinary business expenses — deduct the full amount in the year you pay them on Schedule C. Annual subscriptions are also fully deductible in the year paid. Capital expenses are for assets with useful life over 1 year (like buying a computer) — those get depreciated over time. Your AI subscriptions, cloud services, and SaaS tools are all ordinary expenses, not capital.

instruction: I live with my great-grandfather and code from his house. Can I take a home office deduction?
response: Potentially, but it's tricky when you don't own or rent the space. If you have a dedicated area used exclusively and regularly for business, you may qualify. The simplified method allows $5 per square foot up to 300 sq ft ($1,500 max). You don't need to own the home — regular and exclusive use is the test. However, if you can't clearly define a dedicated workspace, skip this deduction. The risk isn't worth it for a small amount when your other deductions are already strong.

instruction: I filed a provisional patent (USPTO #63/961,403) and paid filing fees. How do I deduct this?
response: Patent filing fees are deductible as business expenses on Schedule C under 'Legal and Professional Services.' The provisional patent filing fee ($160-320 for micro entity) and any related fees (form submissions, correspondence address changes) are all deductible in the year paid. If you hire a patent attorney later, those fees are also deductible. Keep all USPTO receipts and confirmation numbers.

instruction: I use YouTube Premium ($15.24/mo) for research — watching AI safety videos, coding tutorials, and manhwa for my webtoon pipeline. Can I deduct it?
response: Partial deduction. YouTube Premium is a mixed-use expense — you use it for both business research and personal entertainment. Estimate the business-use percentage honestly. If 50% of your viewing is business-related (AI research, coding tutorials, competitor analysis, manhwa research for your webtoon production pipeline), deduct 50% of the annual cost. That's about $91/year. Keep a log or note of business-related channels you follow.

instruction: My paycheck auto-buys Bitcoin and I sell it periodically at small losses. Do I need to report every transaction?
response: Yes. Every buy and sell is a taxable event. The auto-buys establish your cost basis, and each sale triggers a gain or loss calculation. Cash App uses FIFO (first in, first out) by default. Even small losses ($1-20 per trade) add up and reduce your tax bill. Cash App provides a gain/loss CSV — download it and import into your tax software. All crypto transactions must be reported on Form 8949 and Schedule D regardless of amount.
